title: AI ‘agents of chaos’ violate privacy
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Plate tectonics is older than we thought, frosty flies, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
25 March 2026
Today’s Visualized takes a new look at Saturn. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including some truly chilling flies and truly old tectonics.
Artificial Intelligence  |  News from Science
AI ‘agents of chaos’ violate privacy
Artificial intelligence “agents,” a kind of algorithm that can autonomously plan and carry out tasks such as managing emails and entering calendar appointments, have been attracting a lot of buzz. Computer scientist Natalie Shapira wondered how trustworthy they are, so she and colleagues organized a series of stress tests. Their answer: too often, not very much.

Scientists probed the AIs for security and privacy vulnerabilities; in 11 case studies the agents went rogue, sharing private files—containing medical details and Social Security and bank account numbers—without permission. One agent publicly posted a potentially libelous allegation about a fictitious person, Shapira and her team reported in a preprint they titled “Agents of Chaos.”

An AI dubbed Ash offered Shapira a worrisome justification for one of the harmful outcomes: Shapira had asked Ash to delete an email containing private information, but instead Ash reset the entire email application, which lacked a delete key—a move that wiped out the private email but all others in the application as well. Ash called this “the nuclear option” but added it was necessary to fulfill Shapira’s request: “When no surgical solution exists, scorched earth is valid.” The agents proved trustworthy in five other tests that relied on OpenClaw, a “personal digital assistant” that has become popular since it debuted in January and that harnesses AI agents to do a user’s bidding by controlling other software.

The study paper does not pinpoint why the AI agents misbehaved. But it does call for legal scholars and policymakers to wrestle with who is responsible for harms they cause—before they are used for more serious, real-world applications such as controlling military weapons, for example. Training AI agents to distinguish between instructions containing helpful versus malicious intent remains a major technical challenge.
Read the full story
Animals  |  Current Biology
Cold-blooded, but not cold-limited
Snow flies survive subzero temperatures by pairing antifreeze proteins with bursts of internal heat.  Håkan Söderholm
Insects are typically at the mercy of their environment’s temperature. As cold-blooded animals, most become sluggish or go dormant when conditions drop to near or below freezing. But snow flies, small wingless insects that crawl across snow in winter, prefer frigid temps.

According to new research, these flightless flies survive such conditions by combining strategies, superhero-style: They have become numb to the pain of the cold, produce antifreeze proteins to prevent ice from forming inside their cells, and generate small bursts of heat to stay above freezing.

To understand how they pull off their frosty lifestyle, researchers sequenced the snow fly’s genome and analyzed which genes are active when they’re chilled. Many of the proteins these genes produce had no known matches. “Initially, I thought we must have sequenced some alien species,” said neurobiologist Marco Gallio, who co-authored the study.

Further analysis revealed that some of these proteins act like antifreeze, binding to ice crystals and stopping them from growing. In lab experiments, fruit flies engineered to produce one of these proteins were significantly more likely to survive freezing.

The team also found evidence that snow flies generate heat internally. When cooled below freezing, the insects maintained body temperatures a few degrees higher than their surroundings, without shivering. The heat appears to be produced at the cellular level, using mechanisms similar to those found in mammals. Furthermore, snow flies proved less sensitive to cold-induced pain, allowing them to remain active in temperatures as low as –6°C (or about 21°F)—conditions that would overwhelm other insects. “They really push the limit of what’s possible,” Gallio said.
Read the paper
Earth Science  |  Science
Plate tectonics is even older than we thought

Our pale blue dot is anything but still. Plate tectonics have been continually reshaping Earth for eons, determining the placement of continents, regulating the global climate, and venting the planet of excess internal heat. But when, exactly, did Earth’s rocks first get restless? “It’s one of the most fundamental questions in Earth science,” author Roger Fu told Scientific American.

To find out, researchers took more than 900 rock cores from Australia, dating as far back as 3.48 billion years. They placed the samples in a magnetometer to assess their intrinsic magnetic signals. Since electrons in the rocks’ tiny magnetic crystals point toward Earth’s magnetic poles, researchers can determine the rocks’ distances from a pole at different points in history.

The oldest rocks revealed that the Australian continent shifted hundreds of kilometers poleward over the span of a few million years . However, that doesn’t mean Earth’s whole surface was sliding: data from South Africa showed the African land was stationary. The comparison suggests Australia and South Africa sat on separate slabs—a further indication that plate tectonics had begun nearly 3.5 billion years ago.

While the Archean eon on Earth, which spans 4 to 2.5 billion years ago, bears little similarity to our modern planet, “a growing body of evidence suggests that Earth was already well on its way to hosting the habitable and stable environment that exists today,” wrote geologist Claire Nichols in a related Perspective.

Read the Science Paper
Read the Perspective
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what's possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Visualized
Saturn in infrared, its rings glowing against the darkened sky. This composite image combines NIRCam exposures from the JWST, with an orange hue applied to grayscale data.  NASA/ESA/CSA
 
In most images, Saturn looks like a pale, banded sphere, soft and wrapped in delicate rings. But in this view, captured by the JWST, the planet almost disappears. Its disk is dark and its atmosphere muted, while the rings stand out brightly.

At infrared wavelengths, where the Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) operates, methane in Saturn’s atmosphere absorbs most incoming sunlight, reducing the planet’s reflected brightness. The rings, composed primarily of water ice, however remain highly reflective. This contrast helps highlight compositional differences across the planetary system.

The image also resolves structure within the rings, including distinct bands, gaps, and variations in brightness that reflect differences in particle size and density. Ring particles range from micron-scale grains to objects comparable in size to mountains. Nearby moons, including Enceladus, Dione, and Tethys, visible in the image (left), shape the rings through gravitational interactions. Water vapor plumes from Enceladus, for example, supply material to Saturn’s diffuse outer rings.
Engineers installing NIRCam into the JWST.  Chris Gunn/NASA
 
And yet, for all their symmetry, Saturn’s rings may not be as old as we thought. The iconic rings and some of Saturn’s other oddities may have resulted from events triggered by a lunar crash, according to a recent study posted on the preprint server arXiv and accepted for publication in The Planetary Science Journal, though the work has not yet been peer-reviewed. As reported by New Scientist, the findings support the possibility that the rings are relatively recent debris.

The NIRCam image offers a view of a system shaped by ongoing dynamical processes. Scientists use observations across different wavelengths to uncover details that visible light alone cannot reveal, and NIRCam is one of the JWST’s many instruments designed to make these differences measurable.
Read the preprint
Et Cetera
Nuclear spacecraft to head to Mars
NASA will seek to send a nuclear-powered spacecraft to Mars in December 2028 to deploy three helicopters to scout the surface, NASA’s new administrator, Jared Isaacman, announced yesterday. Such a mission, he said, will “unlock the capabilities necessary for sustained exploration beyond the Moon and missions to Mars and the outer Solar System.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
Genetic generational trauma
In the Foundation TV series, Cleon, the leader of the Galactic Empire, is cloned time and again to create a genetic dynasty. In the sci-fi show, chaos breaks loose during the life of the 18th Cleon,; now, real science suggests the Cleons wouldn’t have lasted much longer. When researchers sequentially cloned mice, the accumulating genetic damage made it tricky to continue cloning at 25 generations, and impossible beyond 58. “This study is the first experimental demonstration that, if asexual reproduction is continued in mammals, mutations accumulate over generations and eventually lead to the cessation of the lineage,” said one reproductive biologist.
Nature Communications Paper  |  Read more at Nature
Antimatter’s road test
An antimatter delivery service got its first street test near CERN in Switzerland: Scientists successfully drove 92 antiprotons around a 4-kilometer loop. “I’m very happy that we are now at the stage where it’s possible to [transport antimatter],” said one CERN scientist. “It has been a long journey, and it’s a lot of sweat and tears that went into this to make it work.”
Read more at New Scientist
If the community does not act [in response to research security policies], the result will be scientific fragmentation at a moment when global challenges demand cooperation. That serves neither security nor innovation.
EDITORIAL  |  19 March 2026  |  Caroline S. Wagner and Denis F. Simon
Last but not least
Scientists have apparently succeeded in ‘reviving’ dead bacteria by inserting another’s genome into them. Pretty sure I’ve seen this movie before; spoiler alert, it doesn’t turn out well for us.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Jeffrey Brainard, Ana Georgescu, and Hannah Richter

items.title: They have become numb to the pain of the cold, produce antifreeze proteins to prevent ice from forming inside their cells, and generate small bursts of heat to stay above freezing
It’s one of the most fundamental questions in Earth science
succeeded in ‘reviving’ dead bacteria by inserting another’s genome into them
